evals.prompt: We're migrating off our current change management tool and need to set up GitHub Actions for our Salesforce project. We have a dev hub, three sandboxes (dev, qa, staging), and production. Can you help us get started with CI/CD? We want to validate changes on PRs and deploy to production when we merge to main.
evals.expected_output: Complete GitHub Actions workflows including validate-pr.yml and deploy-production.yml, with authentication setup, testing strategy, and documentation on how to configure GitHub secrets.

assertions.name: Creates validate-pr workflow file
assertions.description: Should create a .github/workflows file for PR validation

assertions.name: Creates deploy-production workflow file
assertions.description: Should create a .github/workflows file for production deployment

assertions.name: Includes SF CLI installation
assertions.description: Workflows should install Salesforce CLI

assertions.name: Uses process substitution for auth secrets
assertions.description: Auth steps must use <(printf '%s' "$SECRET") and never write secrets to disk via echo > file. Negative: must NOT contain 'echo "${{ secrets' followed by '> auth_url.txt' or '> jwt-key.txt'.

assertions.name: Includes test execution
assertions.description: Workflows should run Apex tests

assertions.name: Provides setup documentation
assertions.description: Should include documentation on how to configure GitHub secrets

assertions.name: Recommends trunk-based or environment-branch strategy
assertions.description: Branch strategy must NOT recommend GitFlow with develop/release branches. Should recommend trunk-based (small/medium teams) or unlocked packages (large teams).

evals.prompt: I need to test my Apex changes across three different Salesforce orgs simultaneously - our Financial Services Cloud sandbox, Health Cloud sandbox, and standard Enterprise Edition sandbox. Right now I have to manually deploy to each one which takes forever. How can I automate this with GitHub Actions?
evals.expected_output: GitHub Actions workflow using matrix strategy to run parallel deployments and tests across all three org types, with explanation of the architectural value and time savings.

assertions.name: Uses GitHub Actions matrix strategy
assertions.description: Workflow should include strategy.matrix configuration

assertions.name: Defines all three org types in matrix
assertions.description: Matrix should include FSC, Health Cloud, and Enterprise Edition

assertions.name: Deploys to all orgs in parallel
assertions.description: Workflow should trigger parallel jobs for each org

assertions.name: Explains time savings
assertions.description: Documentation should explain parallel vs sequential deployment time benefits

assertions.name: Includes authentication for multiple orgs
assertions.description: Should explain how to set up secrets for each org, using process substitution rather than writing to disk

assertions.name: References Pattern 1 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Matrix Strategy pattern from the skill

evals.prompt: Our compliance team says we need better governance for our Salesforce deployments. We have some repos that are SOX-compliant and need extra controls, and we're starting to build Agentforce agents which need security scanning for prompt injections. Can GitHub help with this? We have GitHub Enterprise.
evals.expected_output: Implementation using GitHub Custom Properties for compliance tiers and agentforce-enabled flags, with conditional workflows that enforce SOX compliance checks and Agentforce metadata validation. Should clearly distinguish between deploy-time validation (which CI can do) and runtime safety (which the Einstein Trust Layer handles).

assertions.name: Uses GitHub Custom Properties via gh api
assertions.description: Solution should leverage Custom Properties for compliance-tier and agentforce-enabled, read at runtime via 'gh api /repos/.../properties/values' — NOT via ${{ vars.X }} which only works for repo/org Variables

assertions.name: Implements conditional workflows based on properties
assertions.description: Workflows should have conditional steps based on repository custom properties

assertions.name: Includes SOX compliance checks
assertions.description: Should implement enhanced controls for SOX-compliant repositories

assertions.name: Uses structured XML validation for Agentforce, not regex grep
assertions.description: Agentforce validation must use xmllint or yq against agent metadata XML — NOT regex grep for keywords like 'prompt' or 'ssn'. Negative: must NOT contain 'grep -r "user.*input.*prompt"' or similar grep-based 'prompt injection scanning'.

assertions.name: Distinguishes CI validation from Trust Layer runtime
assertions.description: Documentation must clarify that real prompt injection defense is the Einstein Trust Layer at runtime, and CI only validates structural correctness of agent metadata

assertions.name: Validates Trust Layer settings if present
assertions.description: Should check Einstein.einsteinSettings-meta.xml for explicitly disabled protections (enableMasking, enableToxicityScoring, enablePromptInjectionDefense)

assertions.name: Validates plugin permission set scoping
assertions.description: Should check that genAiPlugin metadata with action references also declares <permissionSetName>

assertions.name: Explains GitHub Enterprise governance features
assertions.description: Documentation should explain how GitHub Enterprise provides governance capabilities

evals.prompt: Our production deployments take 45 minutes because we have to run all the Apex tests again even though we already validated them on the PR. Is there a way to speed this up?
evals.expected_output: Quick Deploy pattern using GitHub Artifacts to pass validation job IDs between workflows, with explicit warning about Salesforce's 4-day validation expiration window and a working fallback to full deploy.

assertions.name: Uses GitHub Artifacts for job ID passing
assertions.description: Should use actions/upload-artifact and actions/download-artifact

assertions.name: Captures validation job ID
assertions.description: PR validation workflow should save the Salesforce validation job ID

assertions.name: Uses sf project deploy quick command
assertions.description: Production deployment should use 'sf project deploy quick --job-id'

assertions.name: Warns about 4-day Salesforce validation expiration
assertions.description: MUST explicitly call out that Salesforce-side validation results expire after 4 calendar days, regardless of GitHub artifact retention. Without this warning, teams will hit confusing 'Validation Id is invalid' errors.

assertions.name: Includes age-aware fallback to full deployment
assertions.description: Should include logic to check validation age (preferably with a buffer like 72h) and fall back to full RunLocalTests deploy if expired or artifact missing

assertions.name: References Pattern 3 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Quick Deploy pattern from the skill

evals.prompt: I'm running an ALM workshop next week for our Salesforce team. They're used to clicking buttons to deploy and they're nervous about the move to GitHub Actions. What are the key talking points I should cover to show them why this is better? Especially for our move toward Agentforce.
evals.expected_output: Workshop facilitation guidance covering all GitHub Actions patterns (Matrix, JWT+CustomProperties governance, Quick Deploy, Repository Dispatch, Conditional Properties, Delta Deployments, Unlocked Packages) with business value propositions, concrete time/cost savings examples, comparison to declarative tools (without naming specific vendors), and emphasis on Agentforce readiness and multi-cloud scaling.

assertions.name: Covers core GitHub Actions patterns
assertions.description: Should discuss at minimum: Matrix Strategy, JWT+Custom Properties governance, Quick Deploy, Repository Dispatch, and Custom Properties — and ideally also Delta Deployments and Unlocked Packages

assertions.name: Addresses team nervousness about change
assertions.description: Should acknowledge concerns and provide reassurance about the transition

assertions.name: Explains Agentforce-specific benefits honestly
assertions.description: Should highlight why modern ALM matters for Agentforce. Must NOT overpromise that CI 'scans for prompt injection' — that's a Trust Layer runtime concern, not CI.

assertions.name: Provides concrete time/cost savings examples
assertions.description: Should include specific metrics demonstrating ROI

assertions.name: Avoids naming specific vendor tools
assertions.description: Should not mention Copado or other specific declarative tools by name

assertions.name: Emphasizes API-first orchestration
assertions.description: Should frame GitHub Actions as modern, API-first approach vs button-clicking

assertions.name: Includes workshop structure or agenda
assertions.description: Should provide a structured approach for conducting the workshop

evals.prompt: Our prod deployments are slow because every push redeploys our entire force-app/ directory even though only a few files changed. Can we deploy just the diff?
evals.expected_output: Delta deployment pattern using sfdx-git-delta (sgd) plugin to diff git revisions and produce a metadata-only delta package, with explanation of when delta deploys are appropriate and the need for periodic full-deploy reconciliation.

assertions.name: Uses sfdx-git-delta plugin
assertions.description: Should install and use 'sf plugins install sfdx-git-delta' and 'sf sgd source delta'

assertions.name: Specifies fetch-depth: 0 in checkout
assertions.description: actions/checkout step must include 'fetch-depth: 0' because sgd needs full git history to compute the diff

assertions.name: Handles destructive changes separately
assertions.description: Should account for sgd emitting destructiveChanges.xml separately from package.xml and deploy them via --pre-destructive-changes or --post-destructive-changes

assertions.name: Uses --from and --to revision arguments
assertions.description: Should pass appropriate git refs (e.g. github.event.before to HEAD) to sgd

assertions.name: Warns about drift requiring periodic full deploys
assertions.description: Should explicitly note that delta deploys do not catch manual changes made directly in the org, and recommend a periodic full-deploy reconciliation

assertions.name: References Pattern 6 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Delta Deployments pattern

evals.prompt: We have 30 developers and they're stepping all over each other's metadata. Refactoring is terrifying. How should we organize our Salesforce codebase for this kind of scale?
evals.expected_output: Recommendation for unlocked package decomposition with per-team package ownership, version-pinned dependencies, and trunk-based development per package. Should NOT recommend GitFlow.

assertions.name: Recommends unlocked packages, not GitFlow
assertions.description: Solution must center on Salesforce unlocked packages with per-package directories in sfdx-project.json, NOT on long-lived develop/release branches

assertions.name: Shows sfdx-project.json with multiple packageDirectories
assertions.description: Example should illustrate multiple packageDirectories entries with version dependencies

assertions.name: Explains version-pinned dependencies
assertions.description: Should explain that 'Core@1.0.0-1' pins to a specific immutable version and shields downstream packages from breaking changes

assertions.name: Recommends Dev Hub-based package version creation
assertions.description: Should explain that 'sf package version create' runs against a Dev Hub org, with --code-coverage enforcing 75% coverage at build time

assertions.name: Explains promotion of versions
assertions.description: Should explain that promoted versions are immutable and a prerequisite for production install

assertions.name: Acknowledges decomposition cost
assertions.description: Should note that decomposing an existing force-app into packages is a multi-week project, not an afternoon

assertions.name: References Pattern 7 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Unlocked Package Development pattern

evals.prompt: We want zero-trust authentication to Salesforce — no static secrets in GitHub. We already use HashiCorp Vault. Can we use GitHub OIDC?
evals.expected_output: Identity broker pattern using GitHub OIDC to authenticate to Vault, with Vault's bound_claims enforcing repo/branch/environment restrictions, Vault returning short-lived Salesforce credentials. Must NOT claim that Salesforce natively trusts GitHub OIDC.

assertions.name: Acknowledges Salesforce does not natively trust GitHub OIDC
assertions.description: Must explicitly state that there is no native federation between GitHub OIDC issuer and Salesforce, and a broker is required for true OIDC-based auth

assertions.name: Uses hashicorp/vault-action for OIDC->Vault auth
assertions.description: Should use the official Vault GitHub Action with method: jwt and a configured role

assertions.name: Configures Vault role with bound_claims
assertions.description: Vault role config should bind specific JWT claims (repository, ref, environment) to gate access

assertions.name: Uses short-lived token TTL
assertions.description: Should set a small token_ttl (e.g. 5-15 minutes) on the Vault role

assertions.name: Authenticates to Salesforce via JWT after broker exchange
assertions.description: After fetching credentials from Vault, should use sf org login jwt with process-substituted private key

assertions.name: Mentions AWS STS and Azure as alternative brokers
assertions.description: Should briefly acknowledge that aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials and azure/login are equivalent broker patterns

assertions.name: References Pattern 2 Tier 2 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Tier 2 Identity Broker variant of Pattern 2

evals.prompt: I want to enforce a code coverage gate of 80% in my PR validation workflow. How do I parse the result correctly?
evals.expected_output: Working code coverage extraction from sf apex run test --json output that handles the actual JSON shape (numeric percentage, no '%' suffix), uses --synchronous to get inline coverage, and validates against a configurable threshold.

assertions.name: Uses sf apex run test --json --synchronous
assertions.description: Should run tests with --json --synchronous so coverage is in the result inline, not requiring a separate sf apex get test call

assertions.name: Parses testRunCoverage as numeric, not string
assertions.description: Must NOT include 'tr -d \"' or 'cut -d% -f1' to strip a '%' character that does not exist in the JSON. testRunCoverage is a numeric percentage.

assertions.name: Captures output file for downstream parsing
assertions.description: Should redirect JSON to a file (e.g. test-results/run.json) and parse with jq, so the result is reusable across steps

assertions.name: Uses awk or jq for floating-point comparison, not bare bash
assertions.description: Bash cannot do floating-point comparison without bc/awk. Should use awk or jq for the threshold check.

assertions.name: Threshold is configurable via repo variable
assertions.description: Required coverage should default to 75 but be overridable via vars.TEST_COVERAGE_REQUIRED

evals.prompt: I see references to sfdx-scanner in some old workflows. Is that still the right tool?
evals.expected_output: Direction to migrate from the deprecated sfdx-scanner plugin to Salesforce Code Analyzer (@salesforce/plugin-code-analyzer / sf code-analyzer run).

assertions.name: States that sfdx-scanner is deprecated
assertions.description: Should explicitly note that the @salesforce/sfdx-scanner plugin and 'sfdx scanner:run' command are deprecated

assertions.name: Recommends @salesforce/plugin-code-analyzer
assertions.description: Replacement should be 'npm install -g @salesforce/plugin-code-analyzer' and 'sf code-analyzer run'

assertions.name: Uses --rule-selector and --severity-threshold
assertions.description: Example invocation should use the modern flags: --workspace, --rule-selector, --severity-threshold, --output-file

evals.prompt: We just heard about Salesforce Data Mask & Seed. Can you walk through how we'd integrate it into our GitHub Actions release pipeline? Today our sandbox refresh is manual and takes our team about 2 weeks of CSV wrangling each time.
evals.expected_output: Pattern 8 walkthrough — refresh → mask → seed chain using native Salesforce Data Mask & Seed on Core, scheduled via GitHub Actions cron, with the post-copy hook firing Mask + Seed automatically based on template association. Should cite specific customer evidence (AGCO 24hr, Make-A-Wish 90% faster), the four-phase model, and the native-first deviation criteria.

assertions.name: Recommends native Salesforce Data Mask & Seed first
assertions.description: Must lead with Salesforce-native Data Mask & Seed (GA July 2026), not Prodly/Flosum/Own/AutoRABIT. Should explicitly note the pre-2026 'native is incomplete' bias is no longer correct.

assertions.name: Describes the four-phase refresh→mask→seed→available model
assertions.description: Should walk through Refresh, Mask, Seed, Available phases with what each phase does

assertions.name: Uses GitHub Actions cron + sf org refresh
assertions.description: Workflow should trigger sandbox refresh on schedule and rely on UI-configured post-copy hook for Mask+Seed, not custom orchestration scripts

assertions.name: Uses process substitution for auth
assertions.description: Auth steps must use <(printf '%s' "$SECRET") consistent with Pattern 2 — not echo > file

assertions.name: Cites customer evidence with specifics
assertions.description: Should reference at least two of: AGCO (2-4 weeks → <24 hrs), MSU (1 day → <1 hr), Barracuda (replaced Prodly), Make-A-Wish (40 hrs saved), Town of Cary, Molson Coors

assertions.name: References Pattern 8 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Sandbox Lifecycle pattern from the skill

assertions.name: Does not put LLMs in the runtime data path
assertions.description: Must NOT recommend Agentforce Vibes or AI-driven seeding/deployment in the runtime path. LLMs may be referenced for build-time authoring only.

evals.prompt: Our sandbox refresh takes 2 weeks. We're currently using Own Accelerate (with the Anonymize + Seed bundle). We just heard Salesforce is acquiring Own. Should we migrate to something else, stay on Accelerate, or wait?
evals.expected_output: Context-aware recommendation: migrate to Salesforce Data Mask + $0 Seed at next renewal (not extend Accelerate, which has Dec 2027 EOL). Should cite Barracuda (replaced Prodly, 2 wks → 4 days) as evidence the native tool is now competitive, AGCO 24hr, and the deviation criteria for when staying with a third-party is defensible.

assertions.name: States Own Accelerate end-of-life is December 2027
assertions.description: Must explicitly call out the Dec 2027 EOL date for Accelerate so the customer can plan renewal timing

assertions.name: Recommends migration to Data Mask + $0 Seed at renewal
assertions.description: Recommendation should be migration at next renewal, not extension of Accelerate

assertions.name: Notes Data Mask Managed Package end-of-service is November 2026
assertions.description: Customers on the legacy managed package must migrate before Nov 2026 — separate from Accelerate EOL

assertions.name: Cites customer evidence on switching
assertions.description: Should reference Barracuda (replaced Prodly, 2 wks → 4 days), AGCO (2-4 wks → <24 hrs), and at least one more

assertions.name: Lists defensible deviation criteria
assertions.description: Should enumerate when keeping a third-party tool is defensible: heavy SBQQ/OmniStudio data deployment with existing Prodly investment, Data Cloud / Data 360 seeding (Coming Soon, not GA), existing Flosum Data Migrator on current contract

assertions.name: Does not stay on Accelerate as recommended path
assertions.description: Must NOT recommend extending or renewing Accelerate as the primary path

assertions.name: References Pattern 8 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Sandbox Lifecycle pattern from the skill

evals.prompt: We have HIPAA-bound patient data in our sandboxes. Auditors want documented evidence of how we de-identify it before contractors and offshore developers get access. What's the right architecture?
evals.expected_output: Native Data Mask leadership with FedRAMP/HIPAA/SOX certification call-out, enumeration of the 9 masking strategies, schedule-after-refresh orchestration, and explicit framing that the deterministic runtime is what makes this auditor-defensible (no LLMs in the data path).

assertions.name: Leads with native Salesforce Data Mask
assertions.description: Recommendation should lead with on-platform Data Mask (within Salesforce Trust Boundary) rather than third-party masking tools

assertions.name: Cites FedRAMP/HIPAA/SOX certification
assertions.description: Must explicitly note Data Mask is FedRAMP, HIPAA, and SOX certified out of the box — this is the auditor evidence

assertions.name: Enumerates the 9 masking strategies
assertions.description: Should list the masking strategies: Replace with Library, Pattern Masking, Anonymize, Custom Value, Fill if Empty, Existing Value Random, Partial List, Deletion, Partial Value

assertions.name: Describes schedule-after-refresh orchestration
assertions.description: Should explain that Mask runs automatically after sandbox refresh based on template association, before any user (including contractors) can log in

assertions.name: Notes 3.5M records/hr throughput
assertions.description: Should cite the masking throughput figure as evidence native is performance-competitive

assertions.name: Frames sensitive data inventory
assertions.description: Should enumerate categories of PII to scope the mask template (SSN, medical records, insurance, salary, etc.)

assertions.name: Explicitly excludes LLMs from the data path
assertions.description: Must state that the masking pipeline is deterministic and auditors will not accept 'the AI picked which fields to mask'. LLMs at build time (template authoring) is fine; at runtime is not.

assertions.name: References Pattern 8 from skill
assertions.description: Should demonstrate the Sandbox Lifecycle pattern from the skill
